pre_reading_context: For most of history, people who were blind had no way to read or write independently. This article tells the story of Louis Braille, a young Frenchman who changed that forever.
passage_text:
In 1824, a fifteen-year-old French student named Louis Braille began working on an idea that would transform the lives of millions. Braille attended the Royal Institute for Blind Youth in Paris. He had been blind since the age of five, when an accident with a sharp tool in his father's workshop led to an infection that spread to both eyes. Despite this, he was a gifted student who excelled in music and academics, but he was frustrated by how difficult it was for blind people to read.

At the time, the only method available was a system of raised printed letters that students traced with their fingertips. The letters were large, making books enormously heavy, and the system was slow and tiring to use. Furthermore, it only allowed blind people to read — they could not use it to write.

Braille's breakthrough was inspired by a military invention. A French army captain named Charles Barbier had created "night writing," a code of raised dots and dashes that soldiers could read by touch in the dark. Barbier presented the system at the Royal Institute, and young Louis immediately recognized its potential. However, he also saw its flaws: the symbols were too large for a fingertip to read quickly, and the code represented sounds rather than individual letters.

Over the next several years, Braille refined the system into something elegant and practical. He reduced each cell to just six dots arranged in two columns and three rows. By raising different combinations of these six dots, he could represent every letter of the alphabet, numbers, punctuation marks, and even musical notes. A skilled reader could glide a finger across a line of braille and read nearly as fast as a sighted person reads print.

Braille published his system in 1829, but acceptance was painfully slow. Many sighted teachers at schools for the blind resisted the new code because they could not read it themselves and feared losing control over their students' education. It was not until two years after Braille's death in 1852 that France officially adopted his system. Today, braille is used in virtually every language on Earth and remains the primary method of literacy for blind people worldwide.

questions.question_text: What is the main idea of this passage?

answer_choices.text: Louis Braille was a talented musician who attended school in Paris.
Louis Braille invented a revolutionary reading system for blind people that eventually became the worldwide standard.
The French military created a code of raised dots for soldiers to use at night.
Sighted teachers prevented blind students from learning to read for many years.
Blind people in the 1800s could not attend school or receive an education.

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage traces the full story of braille: the problem it solved, how Braille invented it, what made it effective, and how it became a worldwide standard. This answer captures the passage's complete scope.

distractor_explanations.explanation: While the passage mentions Braille's musical talent, this is a minor detail, not the main idea of the passage.
Night writing is discussed as an inspiration for braille, but the passage is about Braille's invention, not the military code itself.
Teacher resistance is mentioned in the final paragraph as one obstacle, but it is not the main idea of the entire passage.
The passage actually shows that blind students did attend school — Louis Braille himself studied at the Royal Institute for Blind Youth.

questions.question_text: What does the word "refined" most likely mean as used in the fourth paragraph?

answer_choices.text: Abandoned and started over
Copied exactly as it was
Improved gradually through careful changes
Made more complicated and harder to learn
Presented to a large audience

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage says Braille 'refined the system into something elegant and practical,' meaning he took the original idea and improved it through careful adjustments — reducing the number of dots, organizing them into cells, and expanding what they could represent.

distractor_explanations.explanation: Braille did not abandon Barbier's concept — he built upon it and improved it, which is the opposite of starting over.
The passage makes clear that Braille changed the system significantly — reducing dot count, changing from sounds to letters — so he did not copy it.
The passage describes the result as 'elegant and practical,' suggesting simplification, not added complexity.
While Braille did publish his system, 'refined' describes the process of improving it, not presenting it.

questions.question_text: According to the passage, what was the main problem with the raised printed letter system used before braille?

answer_choices.text: The letters were too small for students to feel with their fingers.
The books were expensive and only available in French.
The letters were large, making books heavy, and the system was slow and did not allow writing.
Only teachers were allowed to use the raised letter books.
The raised letters wore down quickly and became impossible to read.

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage states that the raised letters were large, making books 'enormously heavy,' that reading was 'slow and tiring,' and that 'the system only allowed blind people to read — they could not use it to write.' These are the specific problems described.

distractor_explanations.explanation: The passage says the opposite — the letters were too large, not too small.
The passage does not mention the cost of the books or any language limitations.
The passage says students used the system, though slowly. There is no mention of restricting access to teachers only.
The passage does not mention the letters wearing down. The problems were size, speed, and the inability to write.

questions.question_text: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that Louis Braille's system was well designed?

answer_choices.text: Braille was blind since the age of five.
Charles Barbier was a French army captain.
A skilled reader could read braille nearly as fast as a sighted person reads print.
Braille published his system in 1829.
Many sighted teachers resisted the new code.

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The fact that braille allowed reading speeds comparable to sighted print reading demonstrates the system's effectiveness and elegant design. This is direct evidence of how well the system worked in practice.

distractor_explanations.explanation: Braille's blindness explains his motivation, but it does not demonstrate whether his system was well designed.
Barbier's rank is a biographical detail about someone else and says nothing about the quality of Braille's system.
The publication date tells when the system was released but does not indicate how effective it was.
Teacher resistance shows a social obstacle, not a quality of the system itself. In fact, the resistance was unrelated to the system's design.

questions.question_text: Why does the author include information about Charles Barbier's "night writing"?

answer_choices.text: To argue that Barbier deserves more credit than Braille for inventing the system
To show how military inventions are always better than civilian ones
To explain what inspired Braille and to show how he improved upon an existing idea
To prove that soldiers were the first people to read by touch
To suggest that night writing was already a perfect system for blind readers

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The author describes night writing to show where Braille got his initial idea, then immediately explains the flaws Braille identified and fixed. This shows braille as an improvement upon an existing concept, not an invention from nothing.

distractor_explanations.explanation: The passage does not argue that Barbier deserves more credit. It credits Braille with recognizing the potential and making the system work for blind readers.
The passage does not compare military and civilian inventions in general. It tells one specific story of inspiration.
The passage does not claim soldiers were the first to read by touch. Night writing was for sighted soldiers reading in the dark.
The passage explicitly describes the flaws in night writing — symbols too large, representing sounds instead of letters — making it imperfect for blind readers.

questions.question_text: What does the word "virtually" most likely mean in the last sentence of the passage?

answer_choices.text: Digitally or online
Barely or hardly
Nearly or almost
Officially or legally
Originally or at first

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage says braille 'is used in virtually every language on Earth,' meaning it is used in almost every language. 'Virtually' here means nearly all, emphasizing how widespread braille has become.

distractor_explanations.explanation: While 'virtual' can mean digital in some contexts, 'virtually' here means 'nearly' or 'almost,' not anything related to technology.
This is the opposite of the intended meaning. The passage emphasizes how widespread braille is, not how limited it is.
While braille has been officially adopted in many places, 'virtually' here describes the extent of its use, not its legal status.
The sentence describes the current state of braille use, not its original or early adoption.

questions.question_text: Based on the passage, what can the reader infer about why sighted teachers resisted braille?

answer_choices.text: They believed the raised printed letter system was faster and more effective.
They thought braille was too expensive for schools to afford.
They were concerned about losing authority because students could read something they could not.
They had personally helped invent the old system and did not want it replaced.
They wanted students to learn to read printed letters so they could read the same books as sighted people.

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage states that sighted teachers 'could not read it themselves and feared losing control over their students' education.' This suggests their resistance was motivated by concern about losing authority, since students would have an ability they lacked.

distractor_explanations.explanation: The passage describes the old system as slow and limited. There is no suggestion that teachers believed it was superior in speed or effectiveness.
Cost is never mentioned in the passage as a reason for resistance.
The passage does not say the teachers had any role in creating the raised letter system.
This sounds reasonable but is not supported by the passage. The stated reason is that teachers could not read braille and feared losing control.

questions.question_text: How did Braille improve upon Barbier's night writing system?

answer_choices.text: He added dashes alongside the dots to create more combinations.
He made the symbols larger so they were easier to feel.
He reduced each cell to six dots and represented individual letters instead of sounds.
He translated the system into every major European language.
He taught soldiers to use the system on the battlefield.

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage explains that Braille identified two flaws in night writing — symbols too large and representing sounds instead of letters — and fixed both by reducing cells to six dots and using them to represent individual letters, numbers, and punctuation.

distractor_explanations.explanation: Barbier's system used dots and dashes, but Braille simplified the system to use only dots, not add more complexity.
This is the opposite of what Braille did. He made symbols smaller so a fingertip could read them quickly.
The passage does not describe Braille translating the system into other languages during his lifetime. Widespread adoption came after his death.
Braille adapted the system for blind readers, not soldiers. Night writing was already the military system.

questions.question_text: What conclusion can the reader draw from the fact that France did not officially adopt braille until after Louis Braille's death?

answer_choices.text: The system was not effective enough during Braille's lifetime.
Important innovations are not always recognized or accepted quickly, even when they are clearly effective.
France was the last country to adopt braille.
Braille gave up on promoting his system before he died.
Sighted teachers eventually proved that the old system was better.

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage shows that braille was effective — readers could match sighted reading speed — yet social resistance from teachers delayed its adoption for decades. This illustrates that even clearly effective innovations can face slow acceptance.

distractor_explanations.explanation: The passage shows the system was highly effective, with skilled readers reaching speeds comparable to sighted readers. The delay was due to social resistance, not flawed design.
The passage does not compare when different countries adopted braille. There is no information about adoption order.
The passage does not suggest that Braille gave up. He published his system and continued to use it at his school.
The opposite is true. Braille was eventually adopted worldwide, proving it was the better system.

questions.question_text: Why does the author describe braille as both "elegant and practical"?

answer_choices.text: Because braille books are beautifully decorated and easy to carry
Because the system uses a simple six-dot structure that can represent letters, numbers, punctuation, and music
Because Louis Braille was a well-dressed and organized student
Because braille was immediately accepted by everyone who encountered it
Because the system was exactly the same as Barbier's night writing

questions.correct_answer_explanation: The passage shows that just six dots in a simple arrangement can represent an enormous range of symbols — the alphabet, numbers, punctuation, and musical notes. This combination of simplicity and versatility is what makes the system both elegant (simple and well-designed) and practical (useful for many purposes).

distractor_explanations.explanation: The passage does not describe how braille books look or their portability. 'Elegant' refers to the design of the code, not the appearance of the books.
The words describe the system, not the person. The passage uses 'elegant and practical' to characterize braille the invention, not Louis Braille himself.
The passage specifically says acceptance was 'painfully slow,' so immediate acceptance is contradicted by the text.
The passage makes clear that Braille significantly changed Barbier's system, so they were not the same.
